Table of contents

  • Project Setup
  • Tasks for this Assignment

Mod5LabCredit

Author

Maggie Hill

Published

March 26, 2026

Project Setup

Hopefully you have already created a folder for an R project for this assignment.

Save this .qmd file within your new project folder.

The setup code below configures the environment by setting global chunk options, loading necessary libraries, and defining file paths.

Tasks for this Assignment

The Credit data set contains information about 1000 credit card customers. Using this data, you will develop a model to predict whether a person will default on a loan given the values of various predictor variables.

You will begin by considering only a single predictor: balance. Download the data from this site. Create an R project that contains this .qmd file and the data file (in a data folder within the project). Then complete the following tasks.

Task 1: Load the data

Load the Credit data to a dataframe. Check data types to make sure they seem appropriate. Make any needed changes.

Show Code 
creditdf <- read.csv(here(data_folder, data_file), stringsAsFactors = T)

creditdf$Default <- as.factor(creditdf$Default)

Task 2: Perform EDA to familiarize yourself with the data

Put your EDA skills of summarization and visualization to work to familiarize yourself with the data. You should be able to answer the following questions:

  • What is the distribution (shape, spread, typical value) of each variable? Write a sentence describing each one.

For the amount variable, the mean value is 3,271. This variable is right-skewed with a long tail extended to the max value of 18,424. The duration variable has a mean value of 20.9 and is also right-skewed with a long tail extended to the max value of 72. The installment variable has a mean value of 2.973 and is slightly left-skewed with a minimum value of 1.0. The age variable has a mean value of 35.55, so it is left-skewed as it ranges from 19 to 75 years of age.

  • Do any values appear to be outliers or anomalies?

Yes, the amount value appears to have an outlier with its max value being 18,424 despite its mean value being 3,271. The duration variable also has an outlier with its max value being 72 despite its mean value being 20.9.

  • Do any variables appear to be related to each other?

Yes, it appears that the amount variable and the duration variable are related to each other as they both have similar distributions and outliers.

  • Do any predictors appear to be related to the outcome variable?

Yes, it appears that the amount variable, duration variable, and age variable are related to the outcome variable of default. The installment variable does not appear to be related to the outcome variable of default.

Show Code 
#find distribution of the amount variable with skewness
summary(creditdf$amount)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
    250    1366    2320    3271    3972   18424
Show Code 
summary(creditdf$duration)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
    4.0    12.0    18.0    20.9    24.0    72.0
Show Code 
summary(creditdf$installment)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  1.000   2.000   3.000   2.973   4.000   4.000
Show Code 
summary(creditdf$age)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  19.00   27.00   33.00   35.55   42.00   75.00
Show Code 
#find if any values have outliers
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x=amount)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title="Boxplot of Amount", x="Amount", y="Count") +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x=duration)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title="Boxplot of Duration", x="Duration", y="Count") +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
#group sumtable output by levels of Default
sumtable(data = creditdf, group = 'Default', add.median = T)
Summary Statistics
Default
0
1
Variable N Mean SD Median N Mean SD Median
duration 700 19 11 18 300 25 13 24
amount 700 2985 2401 2244 300 3938 3536 2574
installment 700 2.9 1.1 3 300 3.1 1.1 4
age 700 36 11 34 300 34 11 31
history 700 300
... good 36 5% 53 18%
... poor 421 60% 197 66%
... terrible 243 35% 50 17%
purpose 700 300
... biz 70 10% 39 13%
... edu 36 5% 23 8%
... goods/repair 363 52% 132 44%
... newcar 145 21% 89 30%
... usedcar 86 12% 17 6%
foreign 700 300
... foreign 667 95% 296 99%
... german 33 5% 4 1%
rent 700 300
... No 591 84% 230 77%
... Yes 109 16% 70 23%
Show Code 
#example boxplots by level of default
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x=duration, color=Default)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title="Distribution of Duration by Default Status", x="Duration", y="Count") +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x=amount, color=Default)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title="Distribution of Amount by Default Status", x="Amount", y="Count") +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x=age, color=Default)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title="Distribution of Age by Default Status", x="Age", y="Count") +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
# example compare default rates across levels of 2nd categorical variable (stacked proportions)
ggplot(creditdf) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = factor(installment), fill = factor(Default)), position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Default Rate by Installment Level", 
       x = "Installment", y = "Percent", fill = "Default Status")
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = factor(purpose), fill = factor(Default)), position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Default Rate by Purpose Level", 
       x = "Purpose", y = "Percent", fill = "Default Status")
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = factor(history), fill = factor(Default)), position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Default Rate by History", 
       x = "History", y = "Percent", fill = "Defult Status")
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = factor(foreign), fill = factor(Default)), position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Default Rate by Foreign Status", 
       x = "Foreign", y = "Percent", fill = "Default Status")
Show Code 
ggplot(creditdf) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = factor(rent), fill = factor(Default)), position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Default Rate by Rent Status", 
       x = "Rent", y = "Percent", fill = "Default Status")
Show Code 
#example conduct a chi-square test of association between two categorical variables
#install.packages("gmodels")

CrossTable(creditdf$installment, creditdf$Default, 
           prop.chisq = FALSE, prop.c = TRUE, prop.r = TRUE, 
           prop.t = TRUE, chisq = TRUE)

 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|           N / Row Total |
|           N / Col Total |
|         N / Table Total |
|-------------------------|

Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  78.88694     d.f. =  32     p =  7.784572e-06

Task 3: Fit a logistic model

Fit a logistic model to determine whether amount is a good predictor of Default.

  1. Then interpret the coefficient of amount as

    1. the log-odds of default increasses by 0.0001 for each $1 increase in amount

    2. the odds of default increases by .01% for each $1 increase in amount

  2. Generate a plot of the logistic model predicted probabilities over

    1. the given amount values,

    2. a new range of amount values (0 to 50,000)

  3. Calculate the probability of default for amount=$1000 , amount=$1001 , amount=$10000, and amount=$10001

Show Code 
#1
options(scipen = 999)

#fit a logistic model 
creditmodel <- glm(Default ~ amount, family="binomial", data=creditdf)

summary(creditmodel)

Call:
glm(formula = Default ~ amount, family = "binomial", data = creditdf)

Coefficients:
               Estimate  Std. Error z value             Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -1.22937493  0.10833192 -11.348 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
amount       0.00011189  0.00002355   4.751           0.00000202 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 1221.7  on 999  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 1199.1  on 998  degrees of freedom
AIC: 1203.1

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
Show Code 
#2
#a. just use the values of amounts in the original dataset
creditdf$predprob <- predict(creditmodel, type="response")
ggplot(creditdf, aes(x = amount, y = predprob)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Probability of Default by Amount",
    x = "Amount",
    y = "Probability of Default"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
#b. set a range for 'amount'
min_amt <- 0
max_amt <- 50000

# Create a sequence of amounts across the observed range
amount_seq <- data.frame(amount = seq(min_amt, max_amt, length.out = 300))

# Predict probabilities for each amount
amount_seq$prob_default <- predict(creditmodel, newdata = amount_seq, type = "response")

# Plot the sigmoid curve
ggplot(amount_seq, aes(x = amount, y = prob_default)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Probability of Default by Amount",
    x = "Amount",
    y = "Probability of Default"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Show Code 
#3.
#set specific values for amount
new_amounts <- data.frame(amount = c(1000, 1001, 10000, 10001))

# Predict probabilities of default
new_amounts$predicted_prob <- predict(creditmodel, newdata = new_amounts, type = "response")

# View results
print(new_amounts)
  amount predicted_prob
1   1000      0.2464789
2   1001      0.2464997
3  10000      0.4724197
4  10001      0.4724476

Task 4: Model validity

Determine whether the logistic model assumptions are satisfied:

1. Binary response–already checked in EDA

2. Fully-represented data matrix (N/A)

3. Linearity

4. Multicollinearity (N/A)

5. Independence–assume this 6. No large outliers/influentials

Recall that normality is not an assumption, but the QQ plot can still be used to help see outliers.

What do you learn about the linearity assumption?

I learned that the linearity assumption is not satisified as the residuals do not appear to be randomly scattered around the horizontal dotted line at 0. There seems to be a pattern there. The residuals appear to be more spread out at the lower and higher fitted values. This suggests that the model may not be capturing all of the relevant information in the data. I also notice that there are some points that appear to be outliers, which could affect the performance of this model.

Show Code 
summary(creditmodel)

Call:
glm(formula = Default ~ amount, family = "binomial", data = creditdf)

Coefficients:
               Estimate  Std. Error z value             Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -1.22937493  0.10833192 -11.348 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
amount       0.00011189  0.00002355   4.751           0.00000202 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 1221.7  on 999  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 1199.1  on 998  degrees of freedom
AIC: 1203.1

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
Show Code 
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot(creditmodel)
Show Code 
residualPlots(creditmodel, type="deviance", smooth=TRUE)
       Test stat Pr(>|Test stat|)  
amount    3.5304          0.06025 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Task 5: Model Accuracy & ROC Curve

  1. Generate a confusion matrix and accuracy % for the classification rule p=0.5
  2. Generate a confusion matrix and accuracy % for the classification rule p=0.75
  3. Generate an ROC curve for the model and calculate AUC.

Interpret the ROC curve.

The ROC curve helps to highlight the relationship between sensitivity and specificity for various thresholds. For instance, and AUC closer to 1 suggests excellent distinction between sensitivity and specificity. In contrast, an AUC closer to 0.5 suggets that the model is no better than random guessing. Our AUC score of 0.5549 suggests that our model is only slightly better at distinguishing between the two than just random guessing.

Show Code 
# Classify using threshold 0.5
creditdf$pred_class_05 <- ifelse(creditdf$predprob>=0.5, 1, 0)

# Confusion matrix
table(Predicted = creditdf$pred_class_05, Actual = creditdf$Default)
         Actual
Predicted   0   1
        0 691 280
        1   9  20
Show Code 
# Accuracy
accuracy_05 <- mean(creditdf$pred_class_05 == creditdf$Default)
cat("Accuracy at p = 0.5:", round(accuracy_05 * 100, 2), "%\n")
Accuracy at p = 0.5: 71.1 %
Show Code 
# Classify using threshold 0.75

creditdf$pred_class_075 <- ifelse(creditdf$predprob>=0.75, 1, 0)

# Confusion matrix
table(Predicted = creditdf$pred_class_075, Actual = creditdf$Default)
         Actual
Predicted   0   1
        0 700 300
Show Code 
# Accuracy
accuracy_075 <- mean(creditdf$pred_class_075 == creditdf$Default)
cat("Accuracy at p = 0.75:", round(accuracy_075 * 100, 2), "%\n")
Accuracy at p = 0.75: 70 %
Show Code 
#ROC curve
dev.off()
null device 
          1
Show Code 
# Compute ROC and plot
roc_obj <- roc(creditdf$Default, creditdf$predprob)
plot(roc_obj, legacy.axes=T, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve for Default Model")

# Calculate AUC
auc_value <- auc(roc_obj)
cat("AUC:", round(auc_value, 4), "\n")
AUC: 0.5549

Task 6: Model Goodness-of-fit

Use the following measures:

1. ROC / AUC

2. McFadden’s Pseudo R^2

3. Significance test for predictor

4. LRT for null model vs. model

5. Compare Deviance and AIC of model vs. null model

Summarize what you learn.

I learned that the model does not have a very good fit to the data. For instance, the McFadden’s Pseudo R^2 value is very low at 0.01855, indicating that the model only explains a small portion of the variance in the outcome variable. However, the p-value shows that the model is statistically significant, so it plays a small part in painting the whole picture. Our AUC value from earlier of 0.5549 suggests that the model is only slightly better than random guessing between those who default and those who do not. Overall, we have found that the null model vs. model is not a great predictor, so we need to look into other models. The deviance and AIC values are also not very good, which further supports that this model is not a good fit for this data.

Show Code 
mcfadden_r2 <- 1 - (creditmodel$deviance / creditmodel$null.deviance)
print(mcfadden_r2)
[1] 0.01855134
Show Code 
null_model <- glm(Default ~ 1, family="binomial", data=creditdf)
summary(null_model)

Call:
glm(formula = Default ~ 1, family = "binomial", data = creditdf)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value            Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -0.84730    0.06901  -12.28 <0.0000000000000002 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 1221.7  on 999  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 1221.7  on 999  degrees of freedom
AIC: 1223.7

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
Show Code 
library(lmtest)
lrtest(null_model,creditmodel)
#Df LogLik Df Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
1 -610.8643 NA NA NA
2 -599.5320 1 22.6647 0.0000019
